filters.notes: Zone map eliminates ~98.1% of 600 blocks. Qualifying blocks are contiguous. Use linear scan to find block_lo and block_hi.
int8_t comparison, no decode. Applied SECOND in inner loop per iter_0 order.
int8_t comparison, no decode. Applied THIRD in inner loop per iter_0 order.

scan.access_pattern: sequential within qualifying block range only
scan.notes: mmap all four columns without MADV_SEQUENTIAL — do not call madvise at all. Avoids unnecessary kernel readahead on non-qualifying data.

indexes.usage: Load entire zone map at startup (4808 bytes, L1-resident). Linear scan of 600 entries: skip block b if zones[b].mx < 8766 || zones[b].mn >= 9131. Record block_lo (first qualifying) and block_hi (last qualifying + 1). Qualifying block count N = block_hi - block_lo.

data_structures.thread_accumulator: double rev_local (stack variable per thread, no atomics, no padding)
data_structures.partial_array: double partial[N_threads] — N_threads is small (~12), no false sharing concern
data_structures.final_reduction: sequential sum of N_threads thread-local doubles

parallelism.notes: CRITICAL: Do NOT use hardware_concurrency() threads unconditionally. Compute N = block_hi - block_lo (the number of qualifying blocks, ~12). Spawn exactly min((int)std::thread::hardware_concurrency(), N) threads. Thread t processes blocks: for (int b = (int)block_lo + t; b < (int)block_hi; b += nthreads). Each thread handles one or more complete blocks. No partial-block boundary alignment needed — each block starts at b*block_size and ends at min((b+1)*block_size, total_rows). Thread still does per-block zone check (zones[b].mx < LO || zones[b].mn >= HI) as guard. This reduces thread lifecycle overhead from 64 threads to ~12 threads, saving 4-8ms of thread creation and join cost.

inner_loop.description: Per-row logic within qualifying blocks — use iter_0 filter order exactly
inner_loop.notes: Do NOT reorder filters (iter_1 regression). Do NOT split interior/boundary loops (iter_2 regression). Keep the original iter_0 three-filter sequential check order: sd >= 8766 && sd < 9131, then disc in [5,7], then qty < 24.

output.mkdir_strategy: Use mkdir(results_dir.c_str(), 0755) syscall directly — do NOT use system('mkdir -p ...'). The system() call forks a shell (fork+execve+wait = 2-5ms per run). Use mkdir() and ignore EEXIST. If the path has multiple components, create each component with mkdir() and ignore EEXIST, or use std::filesystem::create_directories(results_dir) with the -std=c++17 flag. This eliminates 2-5ms of fork+exec overhead from the measured run duration.

optimization_notes: Root cause is Category D (parallelism tuning) combined with Category A (plan restructuring for output). Two distinct overheads dominate the 19ms run time. First: the current code spawns hardware_concurrency() = 64 threads unconditionally, but the qualifying zone-map range contains only ~12 blocks. Each thread processes ~18,750 rows — trivially fast compute — while thread creation and join for 64 threads costs ~50-100µs each = 6-12ms of pure lifecycle overhead, dominating actual compute time. Fix: cap thread count at min(hardware_concurrency, N_qualifying_blocks) ≈ 12, so each thread handles one or more complete 100K-row blocks. This reduces thread lifecycle cost from 64× to ~12× and eliminates partial-block boundary alignment complexity. Second: the output phase calls system('mkdir -p ...') which fork+execs a shell, costing 2-5ms. Since run.duration_ms measures full binary execution including output, this appears in the benchmark metric. Fix: replace with direct mkdir() syscall. Combined these two changes target ~7-12ms reduction from 19ms toward the 13ms benchmark. Filter order is kept identical to iter_0 (sd -> disc -> qty); no interior/boundary split is used — both alternatives regressed in previous iterations.
